text: An accidental left subclavian arterial injury at the thoracic outlet happened when dissecting the adhesions by the hook.

The subclavin artery at the thoracic outlet is located in the deepest position of the thoracic cavity and is difficult to be repaired in this narrow space once injured, even if the surgery is converted to thoracotomy.
Based on our experience in thoracoscopic vascular injury management, we decided to try to thoracoscopic repair, but we were also prepared for thoracotomy.
First, we immobilized the proximal portion of the subclavian artery and used a releasable endoscopic bulldog clap to block it for subsequent suture.
But there was no space to clap the distal portion of this artery.
It was still very difficult to expose the vascular wound clearly and do the suture without the hemorrhage being completely controlled.
So we had to clap the rupture site directly by using an endoscopic atraumatic vascular clap to stop the bleeding from the assistant port in the ninth intercostal space.

Nevertheless, our first suture failed because of the thorny problem of limited suture angle and the mutual restriction between needle holder and a traumatic vascular clamp, which were both inserted in the same operating port.
The bleeding was even worse after the first suture.
Then another assistant port was made in the seventh intercostal space to facilitate the suture.
where you could control the needle holder to complete the suture more freely with this added port.
Finally, the left subclavin arterial injury was successfully repaired using plagetate suture.
